content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
une 16,
1994
Honorable George G. Alongi
City Councilmember
City of Lake Elsinore
130 South Main Street
Lake Elsinore,
CA
92530
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—94--186
Dear Councilmember Alongi:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding your responsibilities as a Lake Elsinore City
Councilmernber/Redevelopment Agency Boardmember under the conflict—
of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.
In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
‘(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
71.)
QUESTION
As a Lake Elsinore City Councilmember/Redevelopment Agency
Boardmember,
may you participate in the city council’s
consideration of a proposal to build a 300—room Red Lion Inn
(with
a related Conference Center and Marina) at a site within 300 feet
of property you own?
CONCLUSION
You may not participate in any decision concerning the
project if the decision will have y financial effect on the fair
market value of your property.
Of course, whether there will be a
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California ‘Code of Regulations, Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No. A-94-186
Page
2
financial effect on your property is a factual determination that
we cannot make.
FACTS
The city is currently considering a proposal to build a 300-
room Red Lion Inn and a related Conference Center and Marina,
within the city.
You are a Lake Elsinore City Councilmember/Redevelopment
Agency Boardniember.
You also own property immediately adjacent to
the hotel site.
Your property has been developed with
condominiums owned by you and your family members.
In our
telephone conversation of June 14,
1994,
you stated that you own
the property immediately adjacent to the project boundaries.
You have asked whether you have a conflict of interest with
respect to the decision.
ANALYSIS
The Act was adopted by the voters in California by initiative
in 1974.
The purpose for the conflict-of-interest provisions of
the Act was to ensure that public officials, whether elected or
appointed, would perform their duties in an impartial manner, free
from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial
interests of persons who have supported them.
(Section 81001(b).)
In furtherance of this goal,
Section 87100 of th Act
prohibits any public official from making, participating in
‘making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest.
Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:
(b)
Any real property in vhich the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
According to your facts, you own property (part of a
condominium complex owned by you and your family)
which is
situated immediately adjacent to the project site in question.
Your interest in the property is worth more than $1,000.
Thus,


Our File No. A-94-186
Page
3
you may not participate in any decision that will have a
foreseeable
2 and material financial effect on your property.
The Commission has adopted differing guidelines to determine
whether an effect is material, depending on the specific
circumstances of each decision.
The effect of a decision on real
property in which an official has a direct, indirect or beneficial
ownership interest, is material if:
The real property in which the official has an
interest, or any part of that real property, is
located within a 300 foot radius of t1e boundaries
(or proposed boundaries)
of the property which is
the subject of the decision, unless the decision
will have no financial effect upon the official’s
real property interests.
Regulation 18702.3(a) (1).
You stated that your real property is within 300 feet of the
proposed project site.
Since your property is in such close
proximity to the property which is the subject of the decision,
you may not participate in the decision if there will be jy
financial effect on your real property.
3
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By.
) John W. Wallace
J Counsel, Legal Division
2
.n effect is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a
substantial likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not
required..
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is
not reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)
Regulation 18703 provides an exception where the material
financial effect on the official’s interest is not distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally.
(Section 87103; Regula
tion 18703.)
Since you property is immediately adjacent to the
project site, it does not appear that the test for the exception
will be met.
However,
we have enclosed the regulation for your
consideration.
If you do believe the “public generally” exception
will apply, please contact us for further advice.
